title: Insulation options for solid stone walls
subtitle: Breathable vs conventional insulation for pre-1800 rubble, ashlar, or random stonework
excerpt: Solid stone walls are poor insulators, but they are also permeable: they absorb and release moisture. Conventional rigid foam boards trap this moisture and cause decay. Breathable alternatives: hemp, wood fibre, lime-rendered perlite: can achieve similar U-values without the moisture risk, at higher cost and lower insulation thickness.
sourceNotes: SPAB (Society for the Protection of Ancient Buildings) guidance on moisture management in historic solid walls. Historic England Technical Advice Note on internal insulation of traditional buildings. BRE IP 1/06 on hygrothermal analysis of insulated historic walls. CAT (Centre for Alternative Technology) guide to hemp-lime in retrofit. Ecological Building Systems technical data for wood-fibre board lambda values.

glossaryTerms.definition: Insulation added to a wall without a cavity, applied either to the outside surface or the inside surface of the existing wall.
Rate of heat transfer through a wall element, W/m2K. A typical random rubble stone wall is 1.2-2.5 W/m2K depending on thickness and mortar.
A membrane placed on the warm side of insulation to limit vapour diffusion. NOT appropriate for breathable insulation systems in historic masonry.

content.text: A random rubble stone wall 600 mm thick has a
of around 1.2-1.6 W/m2K, worse than a modern 100 mm cavity wall. Adding

is technically possible but the approach matters greatly. Stone walls built before 1919 almost always use lime mortar, which is permeable. The wall breathes: moisture enters during rain and evaporates when the weather dries. Sealing the internal face with conventional PIR board traps this moisture cycle behind a vapour barrier, raises the moisture content of the wall, and over years causes timber decay at embedded joists and lintel ends.
Option 1: hemp batts in a timber batten frame

Hemp batts (lambda 0.038-0.042 W/mK) friction-fit between treated timber battens on the stone face. Batten depth sets the insulation depth. At 100 mm of hemp behind 12.5 mm lime plasterboard, the U-value reaches around 0.35 W/m2K. Hemp buffers moisture: it absorbs and releases without holding enough to cause decay.
with breathable insulation systems. The whole system must allow moisture to move from the wall face, through the insulation, and into the room where ventilation removes it.
Option 2: wood-fibre board direct to wall

Rigid wood-fibre board (lambda 0.040-0.050 W/mK) can be fixed to the stone face with lime mortar dabs. At 80 mm behind 15 mm lime plaster, the U-value reaches around 0.45 W/m2K. Wood fibre is more stable than hemp in damp conditions and needs no batten frame. It costs more per m2 than hemp.
Option 3: lime-hemp render or perlite plaster

A 50-75 mm insulating lime render (hemp-lime or perlite-lime plaster) applied to the stone surface adds insulation with no framework or board needed. Lambda is 0.06-0.10 W/mK. A 75 mm coat takes the U-value from 1.5 to roughly 0.8-1.0 W/m2K. This approach loses the least room depth. It is best used in conservation areas where thicker insulation would need planning consent.

Internal insulation on a listed building does not normally need listed building consent. The outside appearance stays unchanged. If the room-side plaster is historically significant, removing it does need consent. Check with the local conservation officer first.
What done well looks like vs done badly

Done well: breathable materials throughout, no impermeable membranes, lime plasterboard on the room face, joist pockets inspected before boarding. Done badly: PIR foam on a historic lime-mortar wall, a VCL behind it, joists beginning to decay within 3-5 years.
